coredata.dc:title: Design of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on metal organic frameworks and Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica composites for 17β-Estradiol detection
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Sha, Haifeng
Yan, Bing
coredata.dc:description: 17β-Estradiol (E2), an important endocrine disrupting compound, could be quantitatively detected by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) aptasensor, designed in this paper. Metal organic frameworks have large specific surface area and easily modifiable groups, which are helpful for the construction of aptasensor. Specifically, streptavidin was immobilized on the synthesized MIL-53-NH2 by covalent bonding, and further linked with the biotin modified E2 aptamer (apt) through specific bonding between avidin and biotin to obtain the FRET donor probe (MIL-53-apt). Meanwhile, complementary DNA (cDNA) modified Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (RuSiO2-cDNA) were prepared through covalent bonding. They acted as the FRET acceptor probe, since its absorption spectrum showed large overlap with the emission spectrum of MIL-53-apt. In the presence of E2, aptamer modified donor probes tended to bind with E2, owing to their higher selectivity and affinity. Therefore, the optimal distance between FRET pairs was broken, resulting in the fluorescence emission recovery of donor and the fluorescence emission of acceptor decreased. Under optimal conditions, this proposed aptasensor displayed sensitive detection of E2 ranging from 0.5 to 1000 nM with a detection limit of 0.2 nM. Furthermore, the sensor provides a promising method for rapid and sensitive detection of other small biological molecules.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Metal-organic frameworks
Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles
Fluorescence resonance energy transfer
17β-estradiol
Aptasensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence aptasensor for determination of E2
Investigation of fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FMSEM of the MIL-53-NH2 (A) and RuSiO2 (C). (B) PXRD pattern of the MIL-53-NH2. (D) EDX spectra of RuSiO2.

(A) Fluorescence emission (λex = 329 nm) of (a) MIL-53-NH2, (b) MIL-53-apt, (c) RuSiO2 and (d) RuSiO2-cDNA pH = 8.0 TE buffer. (B) The fluorescence intensities of Ru(bpy)3 2+ (a) and RuSiO2 (b) in pH = 8.0 TE buffer (λem = 612 nm). Inset: The corresponding (lower left corner) photos under 310 nm UV-light irradiation and (top right corner) comparisons of the emission intensities.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of RuSiO2-cDNA (a) and MIL-53-apt (b). (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of RuSiO2-cDNA (a) and fluorescence spectrum (λem = 428 nm) of MIL-53-apt (b) in pH = 8.0 TE buffer. (C) Fluorescence spectrum (λex = 329 nm) of MIL-53-apt (a), RuSiO2-cDNA (b) and their hybrid complex (c) in pH = 8.0 TE buffer (Inset: the luminescent images at the 310 nm UV-light irradiation). (D) CIE coordinates corresponding to the fluorescence emission curves of Fig. 3C.

(A) Fluorescence spectrum of this aptasensor with the presence of different concentrations of E2 (0.5–1000 nM) in pH = 8.0 TE buffer (λex = 329 nm). (B) The corresponding calibration curves. Error bars were calculated based on the results of three experiments.

(A) The selectivity of the constructed FRET aptasensor in the presence of different interfering substances (including: 1-NaCl, 2-CaCl2, 3-MgCl2, 4-l-Histidine, 5-urea, 6-glucose, 7-estriol, 8-progesterone, 9-mixture and 10-E2). Error bars were calculated based on the results of three experiments. (B) The reproducibility of three times tests for different E2 concentrations.
The preparation process and detection mechanism of the proposed ratio-type FRET aptasensor.

head.ce:title: Design of a ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on metal organic frameworks and Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica composites for 17β-Estradiol detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Siping Road 1239, Shanghai 200092, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Siping Road 1239, Shanghai 200092, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Liaocheng University, Liaocheng 252059, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical Science and Engineering, Tongji University, Siping Road 1239, Shanghai 200092, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Resonance energy transfer donor (MIL-53-apt) and acceptor (RuSiO2-cDNA) fluorescence probes were synthesized by simple methods. Base pairing between 17β-Estradiol (E2) aptamer and its complementary DNA shortens the distance between the donor and the acceptor, leading to the transfer of energy from the donor to the acceptor. The presence of E2 hinder the process of FRET and make the fluorescence of donor recover and the fluorescence of acceptor quench, so as to realize the ratiometric analysis for E2. The detection of E2 in serum samples shows that the sensor has a practical application prospect, and the method is also expected to be applied in the detection of other biomarkers.

17β-Estradiol (E2), an important endocrine disrupting compound, could be quantitatively detected by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) aptasensor, designed in this paper. Metal organic frameworks have large specific surface area and easily modifiable groups, which are helpful for the construction of aptasensor. Specifically, streptavidin was immobilized on the synthesized MIL-53-NH2 by covalent bonding, and further linked with the biotin modified E2 aptamer (apt) through specific bonding between avidin and biotin to obtain the FRET donor probe (MIL-53-apt). Meanwhile, complementary DNA (cDNA) modified Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (RuSiO2-cDNA) were prepared through covalent bonding. They acted as the FRET acceptor probe, since its absorption spectrum showed large overlap with the emission spectrum of MIL-53-apt. In the presence of E2, aptamer modified donor probes tended to bind with E2, owing to their higher selectivity and affinity. Therefore, the optimal distance between FRET pairs was broken, resulting in the fluorescence emission recovery of donor and the fluorescence emission of acceptor decreased. Under optimal conditions, this proposed aptasensor displayed sensitive detection of E2 ranging from 0.5 to 1000 nM with a detection limit of 0.2 nM. Furthermore, the sensor provides a promising method for rapid and sensitive detection of other small biological molecules.

ce:section.ce:para: 17β-Estradiol (E2), a major sex hormone, is one of the most active mammalian estrogen steroids [1,2]. In recent years, E2 was shown to have severe negative impact on the endocrine system of humans and aquatic organisms as a result of its abuse in contraception, clinical treatment [3], animal fattening [4,5], etc. Intake of E2 by human beings for a prolonged period of time through food chains or other ways, even in extremely low concentrations leads to multifarious and serious consequences, such as abnormal growth, male infertility, and breast cancer [6]. Hence, 17β-Estradiol has become a significantly harmful endocrine disrupting compound [7,8]. Therefore, development of an effective and sensitive method to detect E2 in biological samples is important for monitoring the health of humans and animals [9]. Till date, researchers have developed numerous methods for detecting E2, for instance, surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) [10], colorimetry [11], electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [12–14], and high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [15].

Fluorescence analysis is widely used for detection of ions [16–18], molecules [19,20], biomarkers [21–23], DNA [24] or RNA [25], and tumor cells [22,26], due to its advantages such as simplicity, speed, and sensitivity [27]. Analytical conditions and background signals can interfere with single signal fluorescence detection, resulting in inaccurate results [28,29]. In order to solve these problems, a ratio fluorescence sensor, provided with self-calibration function, which can correct the interference of external factors, has been developed in recent years to increase the accuracy [30–32]. When the emission spectrum of a fluorophore (donor) overlaps with the absorption spectrum of a quencher (acceptor), the transfer of fluorescence energy from the donor to the acceptor, termed as fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), can be observed [33,34]. Furthermore, the mechanism of FRET depends on the distance between the donor and the acceptor [35]. Fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism and ratio detection are often used together to improve the sensitivity and accuracy of the sensor [36,37]. For example, Zhang et al. synthesized an aptamer-conjugated nanocomplex as a self-standard FRET sensor for quantitative detection of exosomes [38].

Aptamers are widely used in the assembly of biosensors, owing to their high specificity, good stability, and easy functionalization [39]. Generally, the key to construct a FRET sensor is to find a suitable pair of fluorescence donor and acceptor. Nanomaterials are widely used in the self-assembly of sensors, due to their unique characteristics, such as quantum size effect, large specific surface area, and excellent optical, electrical, and magnetic properties. So far, sensors based on nanomaterials have been successfully used for the detection of related analytes in food, environment, and medicine [40–42]. Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) have been extensively used for constructing fluorescence sensing platforms owing to their long excited states lifetime, easy modification, and high porosity [43,44]. Therefore, many MOFs-based biosensors were constructed for sensitive detection of multifarious biomarkers [45]. For instance, MIL-53-NH2 (blue-emission) and quantum dots (green-emission) were used for fabricating a ratio fluorescence sensor to selectively detect 6-mercaptopurine [46]. Ru(bpy)3 2+ is one of the most extensively researched molecules used for the construction of optical sensors, due to its excellent luminescence, high chemical stability, and good solubility in aqueous and organic solutions [47]. However, the good solubility of Ru(bpy)3 2+ limits its direct applications in the assembly of sensors. Hence, the immobilization of Ru(bpy)3 2+ is crucial for improving its luminescence properties [32]. Recently, Ru(bpy)3 2+-doped silica nanoparticles (RuSiO2) have gained the attention of researchers owing to their stable chemical properties, convenient modification, and excellent biocompatibility [48]. For example, Chen et al. used CdTe quantum dots to build an electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer platform inside RuSiO2 nanospheres for sensitive detection of α-Ergocryptine [49].

In general, a FRET-based ratiometric sensing platform was designed for the quantitative estimation of 17β-Estradiol. Specifically, MIL-53-NH2 was synthesized by a hydrothermal method and covalently linked with streptavidin. It was further linked to a biotin modified E2 aptamer through avidin-biotin specific binding forming MIL-53-apt, which acted as the FRET donor. RuSiO2 nanoparticles were prepared by reverse microemulsion method and covalently linked to the complementary aptamers after amination. The spacing between MIL-53-apt and RuSiO2-cDNA was shortened when the two fluorescence probes were combined by complementary base pairing. This resulted in FRET, because of the large overlapping of the absorption spectrum of acceptor with the emission spectrum of donor. In presence of E2, the E2 aptamer and E2 have higher specificity than the complementary aptamer. This causes the FRET process to cut-off, resulting in the fluorescence emission recovery of donor and decrease in fluorescence emission of acceptor, thus further realizing the detection of E2.

2-Aminoterephtalic acid (NH2-BDC, 99%) and bovine serum albumin (BSA, 99%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, Tetraethyl orthosilicate (SiO2 ≥ 28.4%) and AlCl3·6H2O (99%) were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Tris(2,2′-Bipyridyl)-dichlororuthenium(II) hexahydrate [Ru(bpy)3Cl2·6H2O, 98%], triton X-100 (99%), n-hexanol, cyclohexane, (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES, 98%) and glutaraldehyde (GA, 25%) were procured from Adamas Reagent Ltd. The E2 aptamer sequence (5′-GCT-TCC-AGCTTA-TTG-AAT-TAC-ACG-CAG-AGG-GTA-GCG-GCT-CTG-CGC-ATTCAA-TTG-CTG-CGC-GCT-GAA-GCG-CGG-AAG-C-Biotin-3′) and complementary DNA (cDNA, 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-GCT-TCC-GCG-CTT-CAG-CGC-GCA-GCA-A-3′), TE buffer (10 mM Tris-HCl; 1 mM EDTA, pH = 8.0), and goat serum were procured from Shanghai Sangon Biotechnology Co. Ltd. TE buffers with different pH values were prepared by adjusting the pH with hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide solutions. Phosphate buffer solution (PBS) (10 mM, pH = 7.4) was prepared by mixing Na2HPO4 and NaH2PO4 according to specific proportions. All chemicals used in this work were of analytical reagent grade. Deionized water was used for all the experiments.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was conducted on a JEOL JEM-2010F electron microscope, operating at 200 kV. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was conducted on Hitachi S-4800. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectra were acquired on a scanning electron microscope, operating at 15 kV. X-ray diffraction (PXRD) patterns were acquired using a Bruker D8 ADVANCE diffractometer, employing Cu Kα radiation source (40 mA and 40 kV). The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an Edinburgh FLS920 spectrophotometer using 450 W xenon lamp as the excitation light source with an appropriate cutoff filter. The UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu spectrophotometer. Fourier transform infrared spectra were acquired on a Nicolet IS10 infrared spectrophotometer. The Zeta potentials were measured using an Anton Paar Litesizer 500.

MIL-53-NH2 was synthesized according to a previous reference, but with slight modification [50]. First, AlCl3·6H2O (493.6 mg) and NH2-BDC (375.6 mg) were mixed in 5 mL deionized water by ultrasonication. The mixture was transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and reacted at 150 °C for 5 h. The yellow product was obtained after centrifugation and washing thrice with deionized water. Thereafter, the product was immersed in DMF and maintained at 150 °C for 12 h to remove any unreacted NH2-BDC. Subsequently, the DMF-treated product was placed in methanol solution overnight for exchanging with DMF present in pores of MOFs and then dried in an oven at 120 °C. Following this, as-obtained MIL-53-NH2 (5 mg) was dispersed in 1 mL PBS containing 100 µL GA and magnetically stirred for 2 h. The product, cross-linked with the amino-group of streptavidin (50 µL, 1 mg/mL), was collected after centrifugation. Further, the E2 aptamer (50 µL, 100 µM) was linked to the streptavidin-conjugated MIL-53-NH2 through avidin-biotin specific binding. After centrifugal washing, 2% BSA was used to block the nonspecific binding sites. Ultimately, the as-synthesized MIL-53-apt was transferred to pH = 8.0 TE buffer and maintained at 4 ℃ for subsequent experiments.

RuSiO2 nanoparticles were synthesized by the reverse microemulsion method [51]. Then, the as-prepared RuSiO2 (20 mg) was added into 5 mL ethanol solution containing 400 µL APTES and stirred for 6 h at room temperature. After several times of centrifugal washing with ethanol, amino group functionalized RuSiO2 nanoparticles (RuSiO2-NH2) were obtained after drying in a vacuum oven at 60 °C. RuSiO2-NH2 (5 mg) was dispersed in PBS (1 mL) containing GA (100 µL) and linked to the amino group of cDNA (50 µL, 100 µM) by cross-linking. The as-prepared RuSiO2-cDNA was redistributed in pH = 8.0 TE buffer and stored at 4 °C for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence aptasensor for determination of E2
ce:section.ce:para: The assembly and detection mechanism of the FRET aptasensor has been illustrated in Scheme 1 . Specifically, MIL-53-apt (20 µL) was mixed with RuSiO2-cDNA (60 µL) for 1 h in 37 °C to obtain a hybrid complex solution. Then, different concentrations of E2 (120 μL) were added into the above solution. After incubation for 2 h, fluorescence signals of the mixtures under the excitation wavelength of 329 nm were measured using Edinburgh FLS920 spectrophotometer.

Serum sample was prepared by ultrasonically mixing goat serum (0.5 mL) with acetonitrile (0.5 mL) for 5 min. The mixture was centrifuged (10,000 rpm) for 10 min, and the supernatant was diluted by pH = 8.0 TE buffer in a ratio of 1:10 and used for subsequent experiments.

To confirm the successful syntheses of two fluorescent probes, several characterization methods were employed in this work. The SEM image of MIL-53-NH2 in Fig. 1 A displayed regular rhomboid structures of about 200 nm sizes. In Fig. 1B, the PXRD pattern of MIL-53-NH2 was consistent with the simulated one, indicating the successful preparation of MIL-53-NH2. The FTIR spectrum of MIL-53-NH2 is shown in Fig. S1. Two sharp peaks at 3390 cm−1 and 3502 cm−1 were assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the NH2 group, respectively. A series of peaks from 1400 cm−1 to 1700 cm−1 could be ascribed to the carbonyl vibration stretching vibrations of DMF, which remained in the pores of MIL-53-NH2. From Fig. S2, it was evident that the characteristic elements of MIL-53-NH2 included C, O, and Al. Furthermore, the EDX results of MIL-53-apt in Fig. S3 testified that the E2 aptamer was effectively modified on MIL-53-NH2 (the additional elements, P and S, were due to E2 aptamers and streptavidin, respectively). MIL-53-apt showed hardly any changes, but its particle size increased to about to 300 nm, after modification with streptavidin and E2 aptamer, as shown in Fig. S4. Moreover, in Fig. S5, the UV–visible spectrum of MIL-53-apt showed blue-shift compared with the MIL-53-NH2, which was probably due to the successful connection of the E2 aptamer with the MIL-53-NH2 through specific bonding between biotin and avidin. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectrum of MIL-53-apt blue-shifted from 463 nm to 428 nm in Fig. 2 A. It could also be seen from Fig. S6 that the E2 aptamer carried negative charge, whereas streptavidin and MIL-53-NH2 carried positive charges. After modification of MIL-53-NH2 with streptavidin, the zeta potential of MIL-53-streptavidin composites increased to 21 mV. When the composite was linked with E2 aptamer, the zeta potential of MIL-53-apt dropped to 11.6 mV. The above results testified the successful preparation of MIL-53-apt.

The as-obtained RuSiO2 nanoparticles showed typical spherical morphology with diameters of 40 nm in Fig. 1C. The elemental analysis of RuSiO2 based on the EDX spectrum is shown in Fig. 1D. The major elements were found to be C, N, O, Si, and Ru, indicating the successful preparation of RuSiO2. As shown in Fig. S7, the RuSiO2-cDNA was still spherical in shape but the diameter increased to 50 nm. This proved that cDNA was successfully attached to the surface of the RuSiO2 without causing aggregation of nanoparticles. Further, the EDX spectrum in Fig. S8 proved the effective synthesis of RuSiO2-cDNA (the extra P element was attributed to cDNA). Moreover, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of RuSiO2 showed three characteristic peaks at 242 nm, 286 nm, and 455 nm, consistent with the Ru(bpy)3 2+ absorption spectrum in Fig. S9. After amination of RuSiO2, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of RuSiO2-NH2 showed hardly any changes. However, when cDNA was crosslinked with RuSiO2-NH2 through GA, the absorption peak at 242 nm for RuSiO2-cDNA almost disappeared. Accordingly, the fluorescence spectrum of RuSiO2-cDNA was slightly blue-shifted from 612 nm to 608 nm, as displayed in Fig. 2A. In Fig. S10, the cDNA and RuSiO2 carried negative charges. After amination, the zeta potential of RuSiO2-NH2 increased to −4.4 mV. After linking with cDNA, the zeta potential of RuSiO2-cDNA decreased to −7.7 mV. The above results testified the successful synthesis of RuSiO2-cDNA. With same mass concentration, from Fig. 2B, the fluorescence intensity of RuSiO2 was 55 times that of Ru(bpy)3 2+, suggesting that the encapsulation of Ru(bpy)3 2+ inside the silica could effectively improve the fluorescence intensity of Ru(bpy)3 2+. This could be due to the increase in number of Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecules doped per nanoparticle.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: A ratiometric fluorescence sensor was proposed for the quantitative estimation of E2 during the FRET process between MIL-53-apt and RuSiO2-cDNA. Following experiments were conducted to explore the mechanism of fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism. When the donor and the acceptor were combined by base complementary pairing, the distance between them was reduced. As shown in Fig. S11, the distance between the two probes after hybridization met the requirements of fluorescence resonance energy transfer. It can be seen from Fig. 3 A that the UV–visible absorption peaks of RuSiO2-cDNA (curve a) and MIL-53-apt (curve b) do not interfere with each other. However, a large overlapping region between the fluorescence emission of MIL-53-apt and the UV–visible absorption of RuSiO2-cDNA was clearly seen in Fig. 3B, proving the feasibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer. Therefore, from the curve c of Fig. 3C, it was evident that after hybridization of MIL-53-apt and RuSiO2-cDNA, the fluorescence intensity of MIL-53-apt was reduced (λem = 428 nm), whereas the fluorescence intensity of RuSiO2-cDNA was enhanced (λem = 608 nm). This further demonstrated the feasibility of the fluorescence resonance energy transfer mechanism. The fluorescent color changes of different solutions were observed by the naked eye using a 310 nm UV lamp (Fig. 3C inset). Specifically, the fluorescence emissions of MIL-53-apt (a) and RuSiO2-cDNA (b) showed blue and orange colors, respectively, whereas the fluorescence emission turned pink (c) after the two probes hybridized. The relevant CIE coordinates from a to b were (0.152, 0.067), (0.607, 0.392), and (0.299, 0.176) separately in Fig. 3D.

The optimal conditions of the key parameters affecting the sensor performance were explored. With a certain mass concentration of MIL-53-apt, the effect of mass concentration of RuSiO2-cDNA on the fluorescence intensity was studied. It could be seen from Fig. S12 that as the mass ratio of MIL-53-apt to RuSiO2-cDNA changed from 2:1 to 1:3, the fluorescence intensity of MIL-53-apt decreased gradually and the fluorescence intensity of RuSiO2-cDNA increased constantly. When the ratio reached 1:3, the fluorescence intensity of MIL-53-apt remained stable, indicating that the combination of the two probes reached saturation. Therefore, the ratio was fixed at 1:3 in the follow-up experiments. Next, the time required for hybridization between MIL-53-apt and RuSiO2-cDNA was researched. As illustrated in Fig. S13, the fluorescence intensity ratio at 428 nm and 608 nm (I428/I608) changed with increase in hybridization time. At 1 h, the ratio reached its maximum, and so the hybridization time was maintained as 1 h for the subsequent experiments. Moreover, the influence of incubation time between E2 and the E2 aptamer on I428/I608 was explored. With increase in incubation time, the ratio increased continuously until it reached maximum at 2 h, as seen in Fig. S14. The effect of pH on I428/I608 was also studied by adjusting the pH value of TE buffer. Fig. S15 showed that the fluorescence ratio was almost same in acidic and neutral conditions, but it increased significantly in alkaline conditions. Therefore, pH = 8.0 TE buffer was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

Firstly, the hybridization of MIL-53-apt and RuSiO2-cDNA was through complementary base pairing. This shortened the distance between the two probes, leading to resonance energy transfer. Subsequently, when different concentrations of E2 were present in the system, the strong specific recognition between E2 aptamer and E2 destroyed the energy transfer process to varying extent. This caused the recovery of fluorescence of MIL-53-apt and the fluorescence of RuSiO2-cDNA was quenched, as shown in Fig. 4 A. The calibration curve with a satisfying linear correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.99573) was acquired between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I428/I608) and concentration logarithm of E2, in the range between 0.5 and 1000 nM. Moreover, the corresponding linear regression equation of the sensor was I428/I608 = 1.29 lg C (E2) + 1.99, with its detection limit (LOD) of 0.2 nM in Fig. 4B. LOD was calculated according to the definition by IUPAC (CDL = 3 Sbm−1), where m is the slope of the calibration curve and Sb is the standard deviation of 20 blank solutions. It can be seen from Table S1 that compared with other biosensors from previous reports, our proposed sensing strategy based on FRET, showed better sensitivity for detection of E2.

Some properties of the biosensor, which can affect its application in real samples, such as selectivity and reproducibility, have been explored in Fig. 5 . Considering the possible interference of substances in serum, we selected the following substances for selective experiments [52]. Herein, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, l-histidine, urea, glucose, estriol, progesterone, and their mixture (all interfering substances with E2) were selected to replace the detection target E2 for fluorescence detection in Fig. 5A. The experimental results showed that the presence of other species in the system had little effect on the detection of E2, suggesting high selectivity of the sensor for 17β-Estradiol. Moreover, three times repeated detection of different E2 concentrations, with relative standard deviation (RSD) from 1.5% to 4.1%, as shown in Fig. 5B, indicated that the sensor had satisfactory reproducibility for the determination of E2.

It is evident from the above experimental results that the proposed sensor has high selectivity and great reproducibility. Therefore, it is expected to show great analytical performances for the detection of E2 in actual serum samples. As shown in Fig. S16, the fluorescence intensity of the sensor changed as the concentration of E2 increased. As seen in Fig. S17, the linear regression equation between I428/I608 and the logarithm of the concentration of E2 was calculated as I428/I608 = 1.46 lgCE2 + 1.77. In the linear range of 0.5–1000 nM, the correlation coefficient and the detection limit were found to be R2 = 0.99184 and 0.3 nM by calculation, respectively. These satisfactory results revealed that the sensor has good prospects for practical application.

Sensitive and accurate detection of estrogen is essential for monitoring human endocrine levels and also for preventing illegal use of estrogen in animal husbandry to promote animal growth and development [53]. A sensitive fluorescence sensor was successfully constructed using FRET mechanism to detect the most active 17β-estradiol (E2) in mammalian estrogen steroids. Owing to a large overlap between the emission spectrum of the FRET donor (MIL-53-apt) and the absorption spectrum of its acceptor (RuSiO2-cDNA), the designed FRET sensor could achieve sensitive detection of E2. The combination of green non-toxic nanomaterials chosen and the aptamers improves the specificity of detection and the stability of the sensor [38,46,48]. Compared with previous single-signal sensors for detection of E2, the dual-signal detection improved the detection accuracy and prevented false positive results [10–15]. The sensor was applied for the detection of E2 in actual goat serum and it showed good reproducibility and selectivity, with detection limit of 0.3 nM. In future, we will continue to explore more applications of the ingenious combination of metal organic framework and nanomaterials for fluorescence sensing.
Haifeng Sha was responsible for conceptualization, formal analysis and writing - original draft. Bing Yan was in charge of funding acquisition, supervision and writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21971194) and the Developing Science Funds of Tongji University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2020.09.030.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Potential bioactivity and association of 17beta-estradiol with the dissolved and colloidal fractions of manure and soil

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.B. Chambers, F.X. Casey, H. Hakk, T.M. DeSutter, N.W. Shappell, Potential bioactivity and association of 17beta-estradiol with the dissolved and colloidal fractions of manure and soil, Sci Total Environ. 494-495 (2014) 58-64.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Establishment of a lateral flow colloidal gold immunoassay strip for the rapid detection of estradiol in milk samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.D. Yang, F.Y. Wang, C.M. Song, S.Y. Wu, G.P. Zhang, X.Y. Zeng, Establishment of a lateral flow colloidal gold immunoassay strip for the rapid detection of estradiol in milk samples, LWT-Food Sci. Technol. 64 (2015) 88-94.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A femtomolar level and highly selective 17beta-estradiol photoelectrochemical aptasensor applied in environmental water samples analysis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.F. Fan, G.H. Zhao, H.J. Shi, M.H. Liu, Y.B. Wang, H.Y. Ke, A femtomolar level and highly selective 17beta-estradiol photoelectrochemical aptasensor applied in environmental water samples analysis, Environ Sci Technol. 48 (2014) 5754-5761.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer/Au nanoparticles/cobalt sulfide nanosheets biosensor for 17beta-estradiol detection using a guanine-rich complementary DNA sequence for signal amplification
